sections.content:

Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.

Red feet at rows 29-31. - Green frame at rows 29-49, cols 41-49 with interior at ~rows 32-46, cols 44-46. - Need to move character toward green frame: ~9 RIGHT and ~5 DOWN from current position.
- Goal model: Move the white character to interact with the green frame (possibly enter it or match its position).
- Action model: UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT likely move the white character. Need to probe.
- Plan: Batch RIGHT moves to approach the green frame horizontally, then DOWN to align vertically.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model.

If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: The character has moved right and is now adjacent to the green frame. I can see the red feet are now touching the green frame. Let me check the exact positions and figure out what to do next.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ('W', 'N', 'R'):
        minr = min(p[0] for p in n['boundary'])
        maxr = max(p[0] for p in n['boundary'])
        minc = min(p[1] for p in n['boundary'])
        maxc = max(p[1] for p in n['boundary'])
        print(f"id={n['id']} color={n['color']} pixels={n['pixels']} rows={minr}-{maxr} cols={minc}-{maxc}")

# Check adjacency
print(f"Adj: {seg['adjacency_list']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I can see:
- White character: rows 14-28, cols 32-46
- Red feet: (29-31, 35-37) and (29-31, 41-43)
- Green frame (N): rows 29-49, cols 41-49 (but now 135 pixels, not 144 - it changed!)
- Red blocks on green frame: (35-37, 38-40) and (41-43, 38-40)
The green frame went from 144 to 135 pixels. This is interesting - the character is now overlapping with the green frame. The right foot (41-43) is at the same column range as the green frame (41-49).
Looking at the image, the white character's right side is now overlapping/adjacent to the green frame. The character needs to move DOWN to align with the green frame vertically.
Current white char: rows 14-28
Green frame: rows 29-49
The character's bottom is at row 28, green frame top is at row 29. So they're adjacent vertically.
The character needs to move DOWN to enter/overlap the green frame. Let me try DOWN moves.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# White char: rows 14-28, cols 32-46
# Green frame: rows 29-49, cols 41-49
# Character is adjacent to green frame on the right side
# Need to move DOWN to align vertically with the green frame
# Green frame center is around row 39, character center is around row 21
# Need about (39-21)/3 = 6 DOWN moves to center

# Let me try 3 DOWN moves first
action(['DOWN','DOWN','DOWN'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
